chunks.text: Randy H. Katz is Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley with contact information including 751 Soda Hall, phone 510-642-8778, fax 510-643-7352, email randy@cs.berkeley.edu, and office hours by appointment via iaortega657@eecs.berkeley.edu. He has a personal homepage, publications list, dissertations supervised, and research support information available. His assistant is Damon Hinson at damonh@berkeley.edu.

Katz was born in Brooklyn, New York, and first exposed to computers in high school. He earned an A.B. from Cornell University in 1976, an M.S. from UC Berkeley in 1978 under Larry Rowe, and a Ph.D. from UC Berkeley in 1980 under Eugene Wong. He was part of the Ingres Database Project. After a postdoctoral year in industry, he was an Assistant Professor at the University of Wisconsin-Madison from 1981 to 1983.

In 1983, Katz joined UC Berkeley's EECS Department, promoted to Associate Professor in 1985 and Full Professor in 1988. He was appointed the United Microelectronics Corporation Distinguished Professor in EECS in 1996. He served as Chair of the EECS Department from 1996 to 1999 and Chair of the Computer Science Division in 2015. In 2018, he became Berkeley's Vice Chancellor for Research, retiring in December 2021. He supervised 52 Ph.D. dissertations and 59 M.S. theses.

Katz co-developed the RAID concept with David A. Patterson and Garth Gibson in 1988. He led the effort to connect the White House to the Internet in 1994. He is a fellow of ACM, IEEE, AAAS, and a member of the National Academy of Engineering since 2000 for contributions to high-performance I/O systems, education, and government service.

Katz has published over 350 refereed papers, book chapters, and books. His textbook 'Contemporary Logic Design' sold over 85,000 copies and is used at over 200 institutions. His awards include the Diane S. McEntyre Award, Jim and Donna Gray Faculty Award, Berkeley Distinguished Teaching Award, ASEE Terman Award, IEEE Mulligan Medal, ACM Karlstrom Award, ACM Sigmobile Award, IEEE Johnson Award, CRA Service Award, and others.

Katz's education includes a Ph.D. in Computer Science from UC Berkeley in 1980, an M.S. in Computer Science from UC Berkeley in 1978, and an A.B. in Computer Science & Math from Cornell University in 1976. His research areas are Computer Architecture & Engineering (ARC) and Operating Systems & Networking (OSNT), focusing on distributed and networked systems design.

Selected publications include works on systems challenges for AI, log analysis, cloud computing at scale, Mesos resource sharing, X-Trace network tracing, packet classification, wireless systems, and the Ninja architecture. Notable papers received awards such as Best Paper, Test of Time, and Landmark Article awards, spanning from 1986 to 2017.

Honors and awards include the Berkeley Citation (2026), Silicon Valley Engineering Hall of Fame (2018), Jim and Donna Gray Award (2012), AAAS Fellow (2011), ACM SIGOPS Hall of Fame (2011), IEEE Mulligan Medal (2010), Diane S. McEntyre Award (2008), ACM SIGMOBILE Award (2002), NAE Member (2000), Terman Award (1999), Tau Beta Pi Eminent Engineer (1999), IEEE Johnson Award (1999), ACM Karlstrom Award (1999), ACM SIGMOD Test of Time (1999), ACM Fellow (1995), IEEE Fellow (1995), CRA Service Award (1995), UC Berkeley Distinguished Teaching Award (1992), AAAS Member (1990), and NSF Presidential Young Investigator (1984).
